title: Unit 02 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Constitutional Principles and Federalism.

questions.question_text: Which weakness of the Articles of Confederation made it difficult for the national government to raise money?

options.feedback: Correct. Under the Articles of Confederation, Congress could request money from states, but it could not tax directly.
Incorrect. The national government could declare war under the Articles of Confederation.

options.text: It could not manage western lands
options.feedback: Incorrect. Managing western lands was one of the powers the national government did have.
Incorrect. The national government could conduct foreign affairs and make treaties.

options.text: Government power belongs only to the president
options.feedback: Incorrect. Popular sovereignty is not about power belonging to one leader.

options.text: Government gets its power from the people
options.feedback: Correct. Popular sovereignty means the people are the source of government authority.

options.text: State governments are stronger than the national government
options.feedback: Incorrect. That idea does not define popular sovereignty.

options.text: Courts have the final say in every issue
options.feedback: Incorrect. This choice does not describe popular sovereignty.

questions.question_text: Which constitutional principle divides government power among different branches?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Federalism divides power between national and state governments, not between branches.
Incorrect. Limited government means government power is restricted by law.
Correct. Separation of powers divides authority among the legislative, executive, and judicial branches.
Incorrect. Popular sovereignty means government gets its power from the people.

questions.question_text: Which example best shows a reserved power?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Printing money is a delegated power of the national government.
Incorrect. Making treaties is a delegated national power.
Incorrect. Declaring war belongs to the national government.
Correct. Running public schools is an example of a power generally reserved to the states.

options.text: A system in which power is divided between national and state governments
options.feedback: Correct. Federalism is the sharing of power between the national government and state governments.

options.text: A system where judges make all laws
options.feedback: Incorrect. Federalism does not mean judges control all lawmaking.

options.text: A system where states have no responsibilities
options.feedback: Incorrect. In federalism, states do have important powers and responsibilities.

options.text: A system where the Constitution cannot change
options.feedback: Incorrect. This does not describe federalism.

questions.question_text: Which is an example of a concurrent power?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Conducting foreign policy is a national government power.
Incorrect. Issuing driver licenses is generally a state power.
Correct. Collecting taxes is a concurrent power shared by national and state governments.
Incorrect. Treaty-making is a national government power.

options.text: A law passed by one state legislature
options.feedback: Incorrect. A state law is not an amendment to the U.S. Constitution.

options.text: A formal change to the Constitution
options.feedback: Correct. An amendment is a formal change to the Constitution.

options.text: A court case about state power
options.feedback: Incorrect. A court case is not an amendment.

options.text: A speech given by the president
options.feedback: Incorrect. A presidential speech does not change the Constitution.

questions.question_text: What does ratification mean in the amendment process?

options.text: Writing the Constitution for the first time
options.feedback: Incorrect. Ratification is not the same as drafting the Constitution.
Incorrect. Debating a bill is part of normal lawmaking, not ratification of an amendment.
Correct. Ratification means formally approving an amendment.

options.text: Explaining a law to the public
options.feedback: Incorrect. Public explanation is not ratification.
